title: Abgoosht (Traditional Lamb Broth)
description: Also known as Dizi, this rustic Persian stew features bone-in lamb, chickpeas, white beans, and potatoes. The broth is served as soup, and the solids are mashed into a hearty paste.

instructions.text: Drain the soaked chickpeas and navy beans. In a large, heavy-bottomed pot (or traditional stone crock), combine the meat, beans, onion, garlic, turmeric, and 6 cups of water. Bring to a boil.
Skim off any foam that rises to the top. Reduce the heat to the lowest setting, cover tightly, and simmer gently for 2 hours.
Add the halved tomatoes, potato chunks, dried limes, cinnamon, and tomato paste to the pot. Season with salt and black pepper.
Cover and simmer for another 1 to 1.5 hours until the meat falls off the bone, the potatoes are soft, and the beans are completely tender.
To serve the Abgoosht in the traditional two-course method: Carefully strain the rich broth into a serving bowl. This is the 'Ab' (water/broth).
Remove the bones from the meat. Discard the onion pieces and dried limes. Leave the meat, beans, and potatoes in the pot.

Using a wooden masher (goosht-koob) or potato masher, aggressively mash the meat, beans, and potatoes together until they form a coarse paste. This is the 'Goosht' (meat/solids), also known as 'Koobideh'.
Serve the broth as a soup course, eating it by tearing small pieces of flatbread (Sangak) and soaking them in the broth (Tilit). Follow with the mashed solids eaten with more flatbread.

tips: Abgoosht is traditionally cooked in individual clay pots called 'Dizi' over a very low fire for many hours. A slow cooker is an excellent modern alternative for this recipe.
storage_notes: Store the broth and the mashed solids separately in airtight containers in the fridge for up to 3 days. Reheat on the stovetop.
serve_with: Sangak bread, raw onions, radishes, fresh basil, and Torshi (pickled vegetables).

substitutions.note: Beef shank with bone marrow works beautifully if you prefer not to use lamb.
